text: Nagel once wrote that the subjective point of view cannot be abandoned without abandoning the subject. This has a practical implication for attention: when we treat our own experience as data — something to optimize, track, or rate — we step out of it. The self-help impulse to monitor one's mindfulness is itself a form of distraction from presence. Buddhist philosophers anticipated this: the Zen concept of 'no-mind' (mushin) is not an absence of experience but an absence of the self-commentary that splits experience into observer and observed. Full presence collapses that split.
question: What does the Zen concept of mushin refer to, according to the passage?

options.text: A meditative state in which all sensory experience is suppressed
The absence of experience itself, achieved through deep concentration
The tracking and optimization of one's own mindfulness practice
An absence of self-commentary that splits experience into observer and observed
